turns.text: Out of those 6000 or 7000 how many goes to a different trial?

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: More than a small percentage and then they haven't plead guilty.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Possibly a bit more than that. I would say closer to 20%. It depends on the section, too. There are some sections where all cases go to trial.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean the section of the judge presiding? That's rather one of the most troublesome a phenomena of criminal justice.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: No doubt about it. Some judges just simply won't talk to counsel --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean to say you --

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: -- in advance about what the sentence is going to be, so they go to trial.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean to say even New Orleans judges play what -- play the judge's presiding to that extent on which --

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Well, if Your Honor please, I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: -- I don't know.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- whether there's a truer version.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: I really don't know. I -- I really don't.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: You must mean by 6000 or 7000 to include misdemeanor?

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Yes. If I -- I had a --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Frankly, we're making surveys all the time, what are load is but it's about that.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Duplantier.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Duplantier is not going to argue in this case, if Your Honor pleases. He is going to argue in the next one.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I see. Before we conclude this case I'd like to ask Mr. -- Mr. Schreiber if he will file a memorandum concerning those cases where this -- where 202 has been departed from as you -- as you mentioned --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: If Your Honor pleases --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- that this --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: -- that is borne out of the -- by the record. The -- Mr. Hubert was mistaken. The case that we cite in here, the Robert Green case, that never did go to the State of -- to the Supreme Court of the State of Louisiana nor did the Alfred Dowels case go to the Supreme Court in the State of Louisiana. These two cases as I recall the circumstances, the Robert Green case was eventually (Inaudible), if I remember correctly. I was out of the District Attorney's Office at the time but if my recollection of it is that it was eventually (Inaudible) The Alfred Dowels case is the case where Judge O'Hara quashed the indictment. Now, those were the two cases where the Court long after the expiration of the grand jury term and as a matter of fact, after the defendant had pleaded to the indictment, had pleaded not guilty, had been arraigned, permitted the withdrawing of that plea of not guilty in order that a motion to quash could be filed. That is cited in the testimony taken in support of -- or our -- our answer to the demurrer filed by the State. What happened in the thing is the -- that we filed our motion to quash, the State filed their demurrer to our motion to quash. We filed an answer to their demurrer. In support of our answer to the demurrer, we took notes of evidence where I testified, Mr. Fust testified and the judge testified. And all of that is in this record.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: The testimony is here. We made it part of the bill of exception that we presented to the State of Louisiana. And it's in that testimony where Judge Hertz says, "I continued the case.” I didn't continue it. When Judge Hertz says you requested the continuance, I had to say well, judge, I'll have to take exception to that. I was on the stand testifying --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, I know that.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: -- I'll have to take exception to that. I didn't continue it. And he says, no. He says, I contended it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: So all of that is part of it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Before you sit down may I ask you this? Have you contended and are you now contending that there's a practice to disregard the three-day limit of which and that this case is a discriminatory failure to apply that (Inaudible)

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: I will --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you contending that?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: I will say with reference to this particular case that this is the first case following the Wilson case, that in my knowledge where a -- a demurrer was filed to a motion to quash of this type. Now, in my experience it does not extend back into the Wilson case. Mr. Becker's experience does extend that far back. He perhaps can answer that better than I can.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I don't know if that --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: This is the first case where they did it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't know what the statute -- the implication of that. Whether demurrer filed or not filed. What I want to know is whether you're contending that this case is a discrimination, meaning by that a conscious refusal or even mechanic refusal by a judge not to apply 202 when the time was up in that effect is, is in all other instances such litigation is allowed. Are you contending that?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: I say it happened. Now, I would not say either Judge Hertz. And I would not say that Judge Hertz deliberately waited up until a certain time he had so forth to appoint me not by any means at all.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not talking about that. It's a question of when he appointed you. The question is assuming that literally, technically, arithmetically, the three days have expired, what I'm asking is whether in -- in -- on every other case or as a matter of habit, the course of business before the criminal courts in this parish, they did not apply 202 as it applied in this case.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Mr. Justice --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you contending that?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: I don't have to make that contention, it happened.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, are you telling me, what I want to know is that in every other case that they disregarded the requirement.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's your position?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: That is right, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you make that position? Did you argue that before the Supreme Court of --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Oh, yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- Louisiana?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Yes, sir. All of this went before the Supreme Court of Louisiana.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you argue there?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That 202 is practically a dead letter?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Well, I -- I -- of course in our practice before Louisiana, we reserved those with exceptions. And we present these bills into -- to the Supreme Court before a review. Then we argue our bills before the Supreme Court. Now, all of the things that we are talking about are part of our bill of exception number one. Now, as I say we reserved 42 bills. Now, whether I specifically call the Supreme Court of Louisiana's attention to this specific thing or not, I cannot say yet at this time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If it -- did you present this as a question for this Court?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do you think that the question that you put on page 4 of your brief in this main argument includes the fact that this is a departure from our norm and therefore the discrimination. And therefore the denial of the equal protection of the law?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: No. If Your Honor pleases, we do not do solely. I might say that with reference to that in that we were not going to urge this question of time before this Court. Last week, Mr. Fust and I, we conformed with reference to this case. And we felt that we would be making a big mistake if we did not urge what, at this particular time, we consider to be a very, very important issue in this case. And unfortunately, this brief was printed by -- by this -- by the -- the Court and we had no time, this only happened last week. We had no time to get another -- issue a supplemental brief and a request that it be printed so that we come up here and we orally urge at this particular time. But it is -- it is raised in our petition for writs of certiorari.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: With this -- I'd just like to call your attention to state the -- the petition for certiorari was granted last February. And your brief was filed here and all these printer form and what the judge said is that between all that time, the reflection on the case, you didn't think of this important point?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: No, sir. Well, it -- it -- we thought of this important point. We did know whether --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't that why he (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: -- we didn't know --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: -- whether -- no, sir, not in our brief. In our petition we have raised it. But we didn't. At that particular time we -- we didn't think that -- that it was important for many reasons. Maybe we just didn't want -- want to raise it because we didn't think we were going to get any results out of it. However --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: -- it is a significant point.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Why does it state here? Now, you think it's a point of great importance. You say you had it in mind but before you -- you rejected the raising of it but now you think it's important.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Well, we didn't --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Why didn't you raise it?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: We didn't reject it. There are a lot of points that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What I mean you didn't reject it so far as notice to this Court is concerned? So far as notice for the State is concerned, you rejected it.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: No, sir, the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you said you had it in mind but you didn't -- decided not to put it in your brief.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Well, that's the only thing. We didn't -- we didn't put it in our brief. Of course, our brief is only a written -- a written argument. And we raise it at this time by way of oral argument. And as matter of fact with the -- the request, the -- that we'd like to make to the Court that we be given the time to file a -- supplement brief covering this particular point, if Your Honors would like to have it by way of written brief.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: (Inaudible) Wilson case?

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Yes, sir, we do.

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: That's inconsistent to this position (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Well, it's an alternative position. It's not inconsistent. It's an alternative.

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: You said yes, that these people have not received equal protection because in the -- they hadn't -- there's been discrimination as between cases isn't it? The rules are unlike, the discrimination of the rule.

turns.name: GERARD H. SCHREIBER
turns.text: Well, this -- this Wilson -- this Wilson case has -- has been a disturbance in the State of Louisiana ever since it was decided. And it is purely, the Wilson case is purely a question of law. We have here -- we are urging at this particular time a mixed question of law and fact in accordance with the judgment of this Court in the case of Powell versus Alabama. We can't prove anything or any of the points raised by Powell versus Alabama without bringing some of these facts in. But we did make them up in the record. We've got them in that -- in that record. Of course, the Wilson case is purely a -- legal question. It's a question of whether the Supreme Court of Louisiana in its interpretation of Article 202, whether it, by virtue of that interpretation has to deny, not only to this defendant but to the defendants coming after him and to subsequent defendants coming after them, the due process of law.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you. Mr. Becker, may I ask you concerning this practice that Mr. Schreiber has spoken of as to -- to the applicability of Section 202. He stated you were more familiar than -- than he is.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes. If Your Honor pleases, I handled the Wilson case. I applied to this Court. My learned counsel on the other side is -- is in error. He read the report that this Court dismissed an appeal. This Court never granted certiorari in the Wilson case. As Justice Frankfurter pointed out, certiorari was refused on the ground that there was no federal question involved in the interpretation of Article 202 of the Louisiana Court of Criminal Procedure at that time which is now the revised statutes of Louisiana. And it's taken right out word for word. There wasn't -- as the Supreme Court of the United States said in refusing the writ of certiorari that there was no federal question involved. That the State had the right to interpret its own statutes as long as that interpretation was not in violation of the constitutional rights of an accused. I think that is the point on this saying. But I might say this that throughout the Wilson case, is the first time that Article 202 was ever invoked against any defendant, white or colored, in Louisiana. That's the only time it was ever invoked to my knowledge after 28 years of criminal law practice. 202 was only invoked for the first time in the Wilson case. Learned counsel on the other side cannot cite a case from the Louisiana Supreme Court in which Article 202 of the Court of Criminal Procedure was ever invoked until the Wilson case. Now, in as far as the procedure which the different justices have asked counsel in Louisiana, we have a system in Louisiana of selecting grand juries and petit juries which does not -- is not the same as in other States. Now, up until the case of Pierre -- Pierre, there were no Negroes ever served on petit juries or grand juries in Louisiana. It was the Pierre case which is the case that came from the 24th Judicial District Court of the State of Louisiana, the Parish of Jefferson, in which this Court forced the State of Louisiana to, at least, put the names of colored people on the petit jury. The State of Louisiana, never at any time, put a colored person on any jury, petit or grand jury. And in the cases of Pierre versus State of Louisiana, this Court forced the State of Louisiana to put colored people on the petit juries. Now, what we are attempting to do is to have the State of Louisiana do the same thing in as far as the grand jury is concerned. And each time that there is a case in which that is invoked, they come up and use 202 since the Wilson case.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: In my -- my humble opinion, I think that the decision in the Wilson case was a decision to deny to Wilson his constitutional rights and of due process of law because if that statute, with all due respect, had been interpreted as written to deny him the right to question the grand jury would certainly have been a denial of due process of law and had been unconstitutional.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. -- Mr. Becker --

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- they -- that isn't precisely the point I asked. Mr. Schreiber said that since the Wilson case, that 202 was not invoked in the manner it was -- has been invoked in this case and under these circumstances. And he cited some other cases and -- and he said inferentially that that was -- it was more or less the practice not to -- not to invoke it in circumstances of this kind. And all I wanted to know, he -- he admits that you knew the practice for those days better than he did. And I -- I just wanted to ask you what that means?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: I don't -- I don't know of any case in which Article 202 has been invoked except about three cases. And strange as it may seem, that in each one of those case, the defendant is a colored person. And each time it's been invoked, it's been attacked. An attack has been made upon the grand jury that filed a motion to quash the indictment because no colored person's won the grand jury. And that's the only time to my knowledge in Louisiana that it has been used. Now --

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: Is that Chianelli?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: In the Chianelli case --

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: He's a white man, wasn't he?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: In the Chianelli case which is a white man, counsel for Chianelli used the -- the decision of the Supreme Court to walk in the morning of the trial and file his motion to quash. That's what he did.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, where was that -- when then did he do it?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: He did it the morning of the trial. That Chianelli was coming to trial --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: -- and the judge -- the trial judge in -- in the Chianelli case said, "Oh, no. The -- I'm not going to follow the decision of the State versus Wilson because that applied -- the Supreme Court of Louisiana was -- was referring to grand juries and not to petit juries and I'm going to overrule your motion to quash this indictment.” That's the situation.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Becker.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Before you sit down --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- Mr. Becker.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, Justice Frankfurter?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- I'd like to ask you why you stated Mr. Hubert was wrong in what he said about the Wilson case. You said he was wrong in stating in this Court found that Mr. -- merely denied certiorari. You were counsel in that case?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You filed an appeal here?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: No, I filed an application for writs of certiorari, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, I have the records before me --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- you filed a jurisdiction of statement as to -- as of an appeal claiming the statute as unconstitutional. The State filed a motion to dismiss and this Court --

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Dismissed.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- appealed from the Supreme Court per curiam, the motion to dismiss was granted.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And the appeal was dismissed. It's a period of the decision based upon a nonfederal ground adequate to support him. I am bound to say that Mr. Hubert was entirely correct on what he said.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Well, I -- I meant to say this, Justice Frankfurter. That there was no writ of certiorari granted that I know of in that case.

turns.name: SHERMAN MINTON
turns.text: Of appeal?

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: It was an appeal.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The point is the Court said there's no federal question in attacking that statute.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: I agree with you. I agree that's what --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: -- the Court said, yes. That's what the Court said in the Wilson case. This Court said that there was no federal --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Until where and what (Inaudible)

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Well, I mean in the matter that he said that there was a writ of certiorari was granted. And then it was dismissed.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, if you don't need to --

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: That's what I meant for the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: -- that he was wrong.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All I'm saying is that he stated correctly what our record show and what your (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: RUDOLPH F. BECKER, JR.
turns.text: Oh, yes. That there was no federal -- federal question involved. That's exactly what this Court has held.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Becker. Mr. Hubert, if you should desire to -- to answer this gentleman, you may feel free to do so.

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: Well, the only point I have -- I have to make is Your Honor asked the question and I don't know that you have a -- a full answer to whether or not -- I think Your Honor's question was -- was this -- it was that same question Mr. Justice Frankfurter had in mind was this an odd case, this particular case. If this was pooled to use against these people whereas it was not used against others. I don't think that's so. Now, of course, it -- it turns out to be an issue of fact between Mr. Becker and myself. He admits that they'll -- three or four he says, maybe it's five or six. If there'd been five or six in the past 15 years, that's normal. It's about what it would be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: How's that, sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The motion to quash (Inaudible)

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: No, sir. And I want to make the point that's been made I think by -- I think it's Mr. Justice Frankfurter. These points were not raised until in this Court. Mr. Schreiber admits that. I don't mean for that that this Court has to stop from considering those points. But I do want to say we didn't know they were coming up. We had no opportunity to answer them. We had no idea. The whole brief that the petition for certiorari does not signal out these issues. Does not signal out that this was a direct discrimination against these people.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I should like to interrupt by saying that nobody would feel stronger against any such particular resolve as (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: LEON D. HUBERT, JR.
turns.text: There's nothing to show that there was such here.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Hubert.
